summary:
The Last Glacial Maximum (LGM) was the most recent period when the Earth's ice sheets were at their greatest extension, occurring approximately 26,500–20,000 BCE. During this period, vast ice sheets covered much of North America, northern Europe, and parts of Asia, global temperatures were approximately 5–8°C cooler than today, and sea levels were roughly 120–130 meters lower than present. The LGM represents the deepest cold of the most recent ice age and the baseline from which the post-glacial warming — the [[event:1262|end of the Ice Age]] — proceeded.

The LGM's end, which is the focus of this registry entry, began approximately 19,000–18,000 BCE when global temperatures began rising in response to orbital forcing (Milankovitch cycles). The deglaciation was not a simple linear warming but proceeded in pulses separated by temporary reversals, most notably the [[event:1261|Younger Dryas]] cold interval around 10,800–9,600 BCE. By approximately 11,700 BCE, at the close of the Younger Dryas, the planet had transitioned into the relatively warm and stable Holocene epoch in which all of human civilization has developed.

The end of the LGM transformed global geography, ecology, and human habitation patterns. The retreat of ice sheets opened vast territories to colonization, the flooding of coastal lowlands destroyed or submerged large areas of formerly productive habitat, and the establishment of new ecological communities created the biomes in which Neolithic agriculture and eventually civilized life developed. The end of the LGM is thus a primary causative event in the chain of circumstances leading to the [[event:1|Neolithic Revolution]] and the rise of complex human societies.
